specification.topic-statement: The REPL completion action system manages tab completion lifecycle including activation from character input, candidate selection via Tab, space-key commit, and dismissal.

scope.in-scope: Completion activation and rebuild on character input, dismissal, Tab key selection and immediate dismissal, space-key commit with trailing space, input buffer text construction for command and argument completion contexts, and original-input preservation across a completion session.
scope.boundary: How command and argument candidate lists are built and fuzzy-filtered from the command registry and agent state is handled by the completion subsystem and is out of scope here.
How the input buffer stores text and manages cursor position is out of scope here; this subsystem only calls into it to read the current text and to replace it wholesale.
How the completion candidate list is displayed on screen is out of scope here.

field.name: current index
field.note: zero-based position of the currently selected candidate

field.name: prefix
field.note: the input text that was used to generate the candidate list

field.name: original input
field.note: the full input buffer text captured before the first Tab press in this session; used to revert on Escape; null until first Tab

behavior.dismissal: Dismissing the completion context frees all associated memory and sets the agent's completion pointer to null. If no completion context is active at the time of dismissal, the operation is a no-op.
behavior.activation-on-character-input:
After each character is inserted into the input buffer, the completion context is rebuilt from the current buffer text.
If the buffer text begins with the character `/`, a new candidate list is generated for the current text as a prefix. Before the existing completion context is replaced, the original-input value is read from it if present and preserved in a temporary allocation.
The existing completion context is then dismissed. If the new candidate generation produces a non-null context, it is installed as the active completion context. If the original-input value was preserved, it is written into the new context's original-input field. If the new candidate generation produces no context (no matches), original-input is freed and no context is installed.
If the buffer text does not begin with `/`, the existing completion context is dismissed and no new context is created.
When the user continues typing while a completion context is active, the context is rebuilt with the updated prefix. The original-input — the text present before Tab was ever pressed in this session — is carried forward into the rebuilt context. This ensures that Escape can always revert to the pre-Tab state regardless of how much additional typing occurred after the first Tab.
behavior.tab-key-handling:
Tab key behavior differs depending on whether a completion context is currently active.
When a completion context is active at the time of the Tab keypress, the following steps occur in order:
1. If the context's original-input field is null, the current input buffer text is captured and stored as the original input. This records the exact text present before any Tab-driven selection modifies the buffer.
2. The current candidate index is advanced to the next candidate.
3. The input buffer is replaced with the text corresponding to the newly selected candidate (see input text construction below).
4. The completion context is dismissed.
When no completion context is active at the time of the Tab keypress, one is created from the current input buffer text and the current input is stored as original-input. The first candidate is selected and the input buffer is replaced. The completion context is then dismissed.
If the buffer is empty or does not begin with `/`, Tab has no effect.
If candidate generation produces no matches, Tab has no effect.
Every Tab press results in the completion context being dismissed after the input buffer is updated. The completion panel disappears after Tab. If Tab is pressed again, a new context is created from the updated buffer text. Because the buffer was just set to a complete candidate name, the next context's candidates will be filtered against that name.
There is no mechanism by which multiple Tab presses cycle through candidates within a single persistent context. Each Tab press creates, uses, and immediately discards a context.
behavior.space-key-commit:
When the Space key is pressed while a completion context is active, the selected candidate is committed and a trailing space is appended to the replacement text.
The input buffer is replaced with the committed text (see input text construction below, with a space as suffix). The completion context is dismissed before the input buffer is updated.
After replacement, the cursor is moved to the end of the input line.
behavior.input-text-construction:
When a candidate is to be placed into the input buffer, the replacement text is constructed from the original-input (or the prefix field if original-input is null) and the currently selected candidate.
The construction rule depends on whether the original text contains a space character:
If the original text contains a space, the completion is for a command argument. The replacement text is formed by taking all characters from the beginning of the original text up to and including the first space, then appending the selected candidate text, then appending any suffix (such as a trailing space for space-commit). The command name and the space following it are preserved verbatim.
If the original text contains no space, the completion is for a command name. The replacement text is formed by a literal `/` character followed by the selected candidate text, then any suffix. The leading slash is always present regardless of the original text.
After construction, the input buffer is set to the replacement text and the cursor is moved to the end of the line.
